source.title: Perceptual boundaries cause mnemonic trade-offs between local boundary processing and across-trial associative binding
source.venue: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition

confidence_rationale: Behavioural evidence that perceptual boundaries (the easiest substrate analogue: latent change-point in z_world) shape episodic memory in a specific trade-off pattern: boundary items get strengthened item-context binding but reduced order memory. The signature is dissociable enough to provide a falsifiability target for MECH-288 substrate behaviour.
failure_signatures: Boundary items showed strengthened item-context binding AND reduced temporal order memory. A substrate where boundaries did not produce this trade-off in downstream memory consumers would fail to reproduce the behavioural signature.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Perceptual context shifts (event boundaries) introduced between trains of stimuli strengthen item-context binding at the boundary and reduce temporal order memory across the boundary. Event organisation imprints structure on free recall.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-288 boundary detection has a specific behavioural signature: items at boundaries should get strengthened anchor-binding (downstream MECH-269 anchor-set consequence) AND items across boundaries should have weakened temporal-order representation. This is a falsifiable prediction for the event_segmenter combined with anchor-set consumers.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Heusser used perceptual category shifts (face vs object) as boundary inducers. In REE the boundaries arise from latent change-points; whether substrate latent shifts produce the same downstream trade-off is exactly what V3 experimentation needs to test.
mapping.source_context: Three behavioural experiments in healthy human adults; episodic memory with perceptual category shifts.

confidence_components.notes: Three-experiment behavioural paper, JEP:LMC. Mapping moderate because the trade-off signature gives an experimental anchor but the substrate's boundary-inducing mechanism is different.
